old_claim.note: Early Firearms is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Paré's 1545 surgical breakthrough was explicitly framed around wounds made by arquebuses and other firearms, making early firearms the historical injury context.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica describes Pare's gunshot-wound treatment reported in La Methode de traicter les playes faites par les arquebuses et aultres bastons a feu (1545).
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source ties Pare's first major surgical publication and wound-treatment reform to arquebus and firearm injuries.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because the injuries were produced by early firearms, the edge records the injury lineage that made the surgical method historically salient.

ontology_before: Surgical Advances (Pare) depended on Early Firearms as a generic historical predecessor.
ontology_after: Surgical Advances (Pare) keeps Early Firearms as the source-backed historical injury context for 1545 gunshot-wound treatment.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: early_firearms remains a direct historical_predecessor prerequisite with textbook evidence.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to non-firearm surgery only.
A source showed the 1545 Pare method was unrelated to arquebus or firearm wounds.
The graph split firearm injuries into a more precise gunshot-wound-treatment precursor node.
short_reason: Pare's 1545 method is explicitly about arquebus and firearm wounds.
